explanation: The input states that as long as V_Mot is 0, Spd_Act must also be 0. In LTL, this is best expressed as 'G (V_Mot_equalTo_0 -> Spd_Act_equalTo_0)', which reads as 'globally, whenever V_Mot is equal to 0, Spd_Act is also equal to 0.' This captures the dependency whenever V_Mot is 0, even if it occurs multiple times or for arbitrary durations. Any previous LTL properties that might use 'until' or 'eventually' would not appropriately capture the requirement that this implication holds at all times, as long as the condition for V_Mot is met.

The input states that whenever (or while) V_Mot is 0, Spd_Act must also be 0. This is a typical safety property that can be captured in LTL as 'globally (if V_Mot_equalTo_0 then Spd_Act_equalTo_0)'. This ensures that at every point in the execution, whenever V_Mot is 0, Spd_Act is also 0. Other possible formulations (e.g., involving 'until') are unnecessary here because the requirement is simply for a global conditional relation, not a temporal dependency beyond each state.

The natural language states that whenever V_Mot is 0, Spd_Act must also be 0. In LTL, this is captured by stating that globally, V_Mot_equalTo_0 implies Spd_Act_equalTo_0, i.e., at all times where V_Mot is 0, Spd_Act is also 0. This guarantees that the listed conditions (here, only one: Spd_Act is 0) are true whenever the controlling condition (V_Mot is 0) is met. No numerical comparisons are used, consistent with the allowed atomic propositions.

The natural language statement specifies that whenever 'V_Mot is 0', then 'Spd_Act is 0' must also hold at the same time. In LTL, this is typically captured as 'whenever V_Mot_equalTo_0 holds, Spd_Act_equalTo_0 holds as well'. This is most naturally expressed as 'G (V_Mot_equalTo_0 -> Spd_Act_equalTo_0)' which reads as: globally, if V_Mot is 0 at any time, then Spd_Act must also be 0 at that time. This properly enforces the requirement for all moments where V_Mot is 0 throughout the execution.

The natural language states that as long as V_Mot is 0, Spd_Act must also be 0. In LTL, this is typically expressed by requiring globally (i.e., at all times), if V_Mot_equalTo_0 holds at some point, then Spd_Act_equalTo_0 must also hold at that point. This can be encoded as G(V_Mot_equalTo_0 -> Spd_Act_equalTo_0). This ensures that whenever V_Mot is 0, Spd_Act is also 0, faithfully capturing the dependency described in the input. None of the atomic propositions are violated and no unnecessary future or past dependencies are introduced.

The natural language statement says: 'While V_Mot is 0, then all of the following conditions hold: Spd_Act is 0.' This means that whenever 'V_Mot is 0', it must also be the case that 'Spd_Act is 0' at the same time. In LTL, this is correctly captured by the invariant globally: G(V_Mot_equalTo_0 -> Spd_Act_equalTo_0). This formula states that at all times, if V_Mot_equalTo_0 holds, then Spd_Act_equalTo_0 must also hold, which precisely models the 'while... then...' relationship expressed in the input.

If we were to use a temporal implication (like V_Mot_equalTo_0 U !V_Mot_equalTo_0 -> Spd_Act_equalTo_0) this would not precisely capture the meaning, as it would not react to changes in V_Mot; instead, the implication at every instant is the most natural encoding.

The input requires that whenever V_Mot is 0 (captured by the atomic proposition V_Mot_equalTo_0), Spd_Act must also be 0 (captured by Spd_Act_equalTo_0). In LTL, this is best modeled with the 'globally' (G) operator: whenever V_Mot_equalTo_0 holds, then Spd_Act_equalTo_0 must also hold at the same time. Formally, this is written as G(V_Mot_equalTo_0 -> Spd_Act_equalTo_0). This translation fully captures the intent that 'while V_Mot is 0, all of the following conditions hold: Spd_Act is 0'. Alternate encodings like G(V_Mot_equalTo_0 & !Spd_Act_equalTo_0 -> false) are equivalent. This logic directly follows the structure of the input. No other previously given property is present.

The natural language specifies that whenever V_Mot is 0, Spd_Act must also be 0. This is a typical 'whenever...then...' pattern, which in LTL is best captured by the globally operator (G): globally, if V_Mot is 0, then Spd_Act is also 0. The translation does not use any numerical comparison operators and simply uses the provided atomic propositions.

The LTL formula is: G(V_Mot_equalTo_0 -> Spd_Act_equalTo_0)

The phrase 'While V_Mot is 0, then all of the following conditions hold: Spd_Act is 0.' means that at any point in time when 'V_Mot is 0' holds, 'Spd_Act is 0' must also hold at that same time. In LTL, this is best captured as a safety property using the 'globally' (G) operator with implication: whenever 'V_Mot_equalTo_0' is true, then 'Spd_Act_equalTo_0' is also true. Thus, we write: G (V_Mot_equalTo_0 -> Spd_Act_equalTo_0). This ensures that at all times that V_Mot is 0, Spd_Act is also 0, as required. No numerical comparisons were used; only atomic propositions are referenced.

The natural language statement 'While V_Mot is 0, then all of the following conditions hold: Spd_Act is 0.' states that as long as 'V_Mot_equalTo_0' holds (i.e., V_Mot is 0), 'Spd_Act_equalTo_0' must also hold (i.e., Spd_Act is 0). This is best captured with the LTL formula G(V_Mot_equalTo_0 -> Spd_Act_equalTo_0), i.e., globally, whenever V_Mot_equalTo_0 holds, Spd_Act_equalTo_0 must also hold at the same time. Other LTL formulae like Spd_Act_equalTo_0 U !V_Mot_equalTo_0 would not exactly capture the requirement, since it would allow Spd_Act_equalTo_0 to cease holding as soon as V_Mot_equalTo_0 stops holding, but might not require Spd_Act_equalTo_0 hold exactly whenever V_Mot_equalTo_0 holds. The given formula precisely expresses the required dependency.
